title: Exploring an open‑source, multi‑market trading terminal through an AI‑trading lens (Neuberg)
author: dogazine4570
selftext:
Not an expert or heavy user here — more of a lurker who likes poking around tools — but I’ve been testing a few trading platforms lately and one of them (Neuberg) made me stop and think about where *AI‑driven trading infra* might actually be heading.

This isn’t a “just launched my product” post. More like an observation from messing around with tools and models on the side.

For a long time it felt like there were only two choices:
- **Retail tools**: easy to use, but fragmented and shallow  
- **Institutional platforms**: powerful, insanely expensive, and opaque  

Neuberg feels like an attempt at a third path: a browser‑based, open‑source trading terminal that connects to *real markets* — crypto perps (Hyperliquid), prediction markets (Polymarket), and US equities (via Alpaca).

What made it interesting to me *from an AI‑trading perspective* wasn’t execution quality, but how information is organized.

### 1. Markets are converging into one “belief layer”
Equities, crypto perps, and prediction markets are all expressions of the same thing: *what does the market think happens next?*

Seeing AAPL price action, election odds, macro events, and funding rates in the same workspace actually makes sense if you’re building models that care about narratives, regime shifts, or sentiment spillover. For feature engineering, this kind of cross‑market proximity feels… inevitable.

### 2. Alpha is increasingly about context, not orders
Execution is pretty commoditized now. The harder problem (and where AI helps) is answering *why* something is moving.

Neuberg leans into contextual data:
- Form 4 insider trades  
- Macro calendars  
- Real‑time news with sentiment tagging  
- A geopolitical heatmap  

This is the kind of data that used to be locked behind desk‑level tooling, and it’s exactly the stuff people end up scraping anyway when building signals.

### 3. Open‑source infra matters more than UI polish
The charts themselves are standard (RSI, MACD, VWAP, Bollinger Bands, etc.), but the more interesting part is that the terminal is **open source**.

For anyone experimenting with:
- custom signal pipelines  
- agent‑based trading  
- model‑driven execution logic  

…being able to extend the terminal as APIs evolve (Hyperliquid, Polymarket, Alpaca) feels more useful than another closed “AI trading app” that won’t tell you how it works.

### Who is this *not* for?
If you only trade one market, want a hyper‑polished UI, or just need fast execution — this might feel rough or unnecessary. It’s more of a sandbox than a finished product.

But as someone interested in how **AI, data, and market structure** are converging, it felt less like a tool and more like a signal.

Less “execution terminal,” more “trying to model what the market believes.”

If you’re curious:
- Site: https://neuberg.ai  
- Code is on GitHub as well

Even if you never use it, I think it’s a useful glimpse into where AI‑native trading infrastructure might be heading.

comments.author: Live-Bag-1775
comments.body:
Interesting framing—“belief layer” is a good way to put it.

Feels like tools like this are more valuable for feature engineering than execution. Curious if anyone’s actually turning that context into stable, out-of-sample alpha.

comments.author: laxflo
comments.body: Pretty neat! And very well done, thanks for sharing.
